name: HumanMetagenomeDB

relevance.justification: Contains per-sample demographics for all four required fields (age, sex/gender, BMI, ethnicity) across 69,822 samples, and 58 phenotype/disease diagnoses (+2 each). Data is structured/tabular with 203 standardized CSV-exportable fields (+1). Uses SRA/BioProject/PubMed identifiers and MIMS v5.0 standards (+1). Key gap: the DB itself stores metadata and raw-sequence accessions only — it does NOT contain precomputed species-level taxonomic abundance tables. Species-level microbiome abundance data must be derived from the linked SRA raw reads by the user (score 0 for Q1). Maintenance status uncertain: last versioned release was v1.1 June 2020; site is live but no new version documented (+0 for Q5).

novelty.justification: No existing BioThings API covers standardized human metagenome sample-level metadata at this scale (69,822 samples, 203 fields, 58 countries, 9 body sites, 58 diagnoses). DisGeNET/HPO cover disease-gene associations, not metagenome sample metadata. The standardized demographic + phenotype metadata layer (age, BMI, sex, ethnicity, diet, chemical exposure, geographic coordinates) is genuinely novel for BioThings. Slight deduction from 5: the DB is a metadata aggregator over SRA/MG-RAST, which are themselves already indexable, so the value is the curation layer, not raw data novelty.

openness.justification: Published under CC BY 4.0 (Oxford University Press Open Access) — redistribution permitted with attribution. Web application at web.app.ufz.de/hmgdb/ is publicly accessible without login and offers CSV export of filtered metadata. However, no static bulk download URL for the full dataset was found — access is via interactive Shiny app only. The GitHub repo (mdsufz/hmgdb_script) provides a downloader for raw sequences, not the metadata CSV. Cap at 3: open with attribution, but absence of a direct static bulk URL is a minor access barrier for automated ingestion.

evidence.scope: Standardized metadata for 69,822 human metagenome samples from 580 independent studies. Covers 58 countries, 9 body sites (gut 63.5%, oral 19%, skin, ear, lung, vagina, bone, biofluids), 58 diagnoses, and 203 metadata attributes per sample. Data aggregated from SRA (95.4%) and MG-RAST (4.6%). Does NOT contain precomputed taxonomic abundance tables — raw sequences must be downloaded separately.
evidence.overlap: No direct overlap with existing BioThings APIs. SRA accessions are referenced by NCBI but no BioThings API currently indexes standardized metagenome sample-level demographic metadata at this scale.

urls.api: https://web.app.ufz.de/hmgdb/ (Shiny web app — CSV export via UI, no REST API documented)

api_info.record_coverage: Metadata CSV export available via Shiny UI; no programmatic REST/GraphQL API documented. Raw sequence download via hmgdb_downloader.py requires user-provided metadata CSV from the web app.

risks: No static bulk download URL — full metadata table must be exported manually through the Shiny web app; blocks automated ingestion pipeline without a scraping step or direct DB access
Last versioned release was v1.1 (June 2020); maintenance activity post-2020 unverified — dataset may be stale relative to current SRA holdings
DB stores metadata only, not precomputed taxonomic profiles; species-level microbiome data requires user to download and process raw sequences from SRA/MG-RAST
Semi-automatic standardization with manual curation bottleneck — update cadence (twice/year) may lag behind SRA submission rates
next_actions: Contact HMgDB authors or UFZ team to request a static bulk metadata dump URL or database snapshot (SQL/CSV) for automated ingestion
Check if a full metadata export is embedded in the Shiny app source or available via the UFZ data repository (data.ufz.de)
If bulk download confirmed: proceed to site inspection to document exact field names and formats for plugin mapping
Consider scoping ingestion to metadata only (no raw sequence processing) — demographic + phenotype layer is the BioThings-relevant artifact
Re-evaluate relevance score if a precomputed species-level abundance table (e.g., from EBI MGnify linked studies) can be co-ingested
